summary: This page covers stick (SMAW) welding troubleshooting for common bead defects: slag coating, porosity, crooked/wavy beads, excessive spatter, and burn-through. Each section includes a top-view diagram of the weld bead and a list of possible causes with recommended solutions.
text:
Stick Weld–Coat of Slag Over Weld
TOP VIEW
PARTIALLY CHIPPED AWAY TO SHOW WELD
Slag is a necessary part of a stick weld. It shields the weld from impurities. Clean off the slag with the Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush after welding.

Stick Weld–Porosity — Small cavities or holes in the bead.
POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS
TOP VIEW
1. Dirty workpiece or fill material: Clean workpiece down to bare metal. Make certain that fill material and Electrode are clean and free from oil, coatings, and other residues.
2. Inconsistent welding speed: Maintain steady weld speed.

Stick Weld–Crooked/Wavy Bead
POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS
TOP VIEW
1. Inaccurate welding: Use two hands or rest hand on steady surface.
2. Inconsistent welding speed: Maintain steady weld speed.

Stick Weld–Excessive Spatter
POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS
TOP VIEW
Fine spatter is normal. Spatter that is grainy and large is a problem.
Dirty workpiece or fill material: Clean workpiece down to bare metal. Make certain that fill material and Electrode are clean and free from oil, coatings, and other residues.

Stick Weld–Burn-Through — Base material melts away, leaving a hole in the weld.
POSSIBLE CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS
TOP VIEW
1. Workpiece overheating: Reduce current.
2. Welding speed too slow: Increase welding speed and ensure that welding speed is kept steady.
3. Excessive material at weld: Reduce amount of fill material.

Page 40. For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353. Item 57812

figures.caption: Stick Weld–Coat of Slag Over Weld (TOP VIEW)
figures.description: Top view illustration of a stick weld bead partially covered in a dark slag coating with the slag chipped away on part of it to reveal the underlying weld bead texture.

figures.caption: Stick Weld–Porosity (TOP VIEW)
figures.description: Top view of a weld bead showing scattered small white dots representing cavities or holes (porosity) along the bead surface.

figures.caption: Stick Weld–Crooked/Wavy Bead (TOP VIEW)
figures.description: Top view of a weld bead that curves and waves irregularly rather than running in a straight line.

figures.caption: Stick Weld–Excessive Spatter (TOP VIEW)
figures.description: Top view of a weld bead surrounded by scattered dark specks representing spatter droplets on the surrounding surface.

figures.caption: Stick Weld–Burn-Through (TOP VIEW)
figures.description: Top view of a weld bead with a large white hole/gap where the base material has melted away, illustrating burn-through.

key_facts: Slag is a necessary part of a stick weld and shields the weld from impurities; clean it off with a Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush after welding.

Porosity (small cavities or holes in the bead) is caused by dirty workpiece/fill material or inconsistent welding speed; solutions are cleaning workpiece to bare metal, ensuring fill material and electrode are free of oil/coatings/residue, and maintaining steady weld speed.
Crooked/wavy bead is caused by inaccurate welding (fix: use two hands or rest hand on steady surface) or inconsistent welding speed (fix: maintain steady speed).

Fine spatter during stick welding is normal; grainy, large spatter is a problem caused by dirty workpiece or fill material, fixed by cleaning workpiece to bare metal and ensuring fill material/electrode are free of oil, coatings, and residues.

Burn-through (base material melts away leaving a hole) is caused by workpiece overheating (reduce current), welding speed too slow (increase and steady welding speed), or excessive material at weld (reduce fill material amount).
